metadata.preview:
# AGENTS.md — Marketing Hub (marketing-hub)

Marketing Hub — All-in-One Marketing Command Center. A Next.js + React application.

## Stack
- Next.js + React (28 dependencies)
- `next.config.js`, `components.json` (shadcn-style UI), Capacitor (`capacitor.config.json`
  → mobile packaging), Tailwind/CSS
- `data/` and `docs/` hold content and documentation

## Common commands
- `npm run dev` — local dev server
- `npm run build` — production build
- `npm run start` — serve the production build
- `npm run lint` — lint
- `npm run seed` — seed local data

## Notes for the coding agent (pi)
- pi is the coding agent for this project.
- **The working tree is heavily modified (~119 uncommitted changes).** Do NOT run

state.title: marketing-hub/AGENTS.md

state.output:
<path>/home/uzer/marketing-hub/README.md</path>
<type>file</type>
<content>
1: # Marketing Hub — All-in-One Marketing Command Center
2: 
3: > ⚡ **Core Doctrine: Intensity Beats Extensity — Every Time.**
4: > One channel mastered beats five channels touched. One offer sharpened beats ten offers scattered. One persona deeply understood beats five personas guessed. Read the full principle in [`docs/INTENSITY_PRINCIPLE.md`](docs/INTENSITY_PRINCIPLE.md).
5: 
6: A complete marketing platform built with **Next.js 14, TypeScript, Tailwind, and SQLite**. **30 interconnected sections** covering the entire marketing workflow — all running locally, no external API keys required.
7: 
8: ## Quick start
9: 
10: ```bash
11: cd ~/marketing-hub
12: npm install
13: npm run seed               # populate with 200+ sample records
14: npm run dev                # http://localhost:3000
15: ```
16: 
17: For production:
18: ```bash
19: npm run build
20: npm start
21: ```
22: 
23: ## What's inside (30 sections)
24: 
25: ### 🧭 Plan
26: | Section | What it does |
27: |---|---|
28: | **Dashboard** | Unified metrics, module grid, traffic/conversion charts, active campaigns, upcoming content |
29: | **Brand kit** | Logos (primary/dark/icon), color palette, typography, voice & tone, usage guidelines |
30: | **Personas** | Audience profiles with goals, pain points, channels, messaging. Primary / secondary / anti-persona tiers |
31: | **Segments** | Rule-based audience segments (e.g. `score > 70 AND tag = newsletter`) |
32: | **Competitors** | Track competitors: positioning, pricing, SWOT analysis, strengths & weaknesses |
33: | **Campaigns** | Kanban (draft → scheduled → live → paused → completed), full CRUD, detail page, budget tracking |
34: | **Funnels** | Visual customer-journey builder — map awareness → landing → form → email → sale. Tracks conversion per step |
35: | **Budget planner** | Monthly budget by category, planned vs actual, variance tracking, category breakdown chart |
36: | **Playbooks** | Repeatable SOPs with steps, owners, durations, KPIs. Acquisition / activation / retention / revenue / branding |
37: 
38: ### ✏️ Create
39: | Section | What it does |
40: |---|---|
41: | **Content calendar** | Monthly grid view with per-channel color coding, click-to-create |
42: | **Social studio** | Composer with per-channel character limits (Twitter 280, LinkedIn 3000, etc.), preview, scheduling |
43: | **Email builder** | Block-based editor (hero, features, CTA, testimonials, pricing, FAQ), live preview, HTML/text export |
44: | **Landing pages** | Same block editor for marketing pages, live preview, SEO meta, static HTML export |
45: | **Forms** | Drag-style form builder — text/email/phone/textarea/select/checkbox/number/URL fields, submit tracking |
46: | **Lead magnets** | Library of eBooks, templates, checklists, webinars, coupons, videos — track downloads |
47: | **Copy generator** | Offline template-based: headlines, hooks, CTAs, ad copy, social posts, email sequences, blog outlines |
48: | **Asset library** | File upload, image/video/document previews, search, tags |
49: 
50: ### 📈 Grow
51: | Section | What it does |
52: |---|---|
53: | **SEO toolkit** | Keyword tracker (volume/difficulty/opportunity), HTML auditor, sitemap.xml, robots.txt |
54: | **Ads manager** | Multi-channel (Meta/Google/TikTok/LinkedIn), spend/impressions/clicks/CTR/CPA, pause/resume |
55: | **Events & webinars** | Webinars, conferences, meetups, workshops, launches — registration tracking, capacity fill rate |
56: | **CRM & deals** | Contacts with scoring, companies, deals kanban (drag between stages), activity tracking |
57: | **Automations** | Trigger→action rule builder (8 triggers × 8 actions), run tracking, pause/resume |
58: | **A/B testing** | Experiments with two variants, traffic & conversions, auto-computed lift & confidence, winner flag |
59: | **Testimonials** | Social proof library with ratings, sources (G2/Trustpilot/Twitter/email/manual), featured toggle, avatars |
60: | **Conversations inbox** | Unified inbox — DMs, comments, replies, emails. Channel filters, sentiment badges, star, search, reply |
61: | **Surveys (NPS, CSAT)** | NPS (0-10), CSAT (1-5), CES (1-7), custom — track responses & aggregate score |
62: | **Analytics** | Multi-range charts (traffic/conversions/revenue), funnel viz, channel mix, per-channel performance |
63: 
64: ### ⚙️ Manage
65: | Section | What it does |
66: |---|---|
67: | **Tasks** | To-do tracker with priority, due date, project tagging |
68: | **Integrations** | 24 service connectors (Mailchimp, SendGrid, HubSpot, Meta, Google, Slack, etc.) with connect/disconnect flow |
69: | **Reports** | Auto-generated performance summaries, CSV export, jsPDF PDF export |
70: | **Settings** | Workspace, brand color, email defaults, API key vault (encrypted at rest) |
71: 
72: ## Architecture
73: 
74: - **Next.js 14 App Router** with server components + client islands
75: - **SQLite** (`better-sqlite3`) for everything — single-file database at `data/hub.sqlite`
76: - **Tailwind CSS** + custom design system (no UI library), dynamic color safelist
77: - **Recharts** for all charts
78: - **lucide-react** for icons
79: - **framer-motion** for premium animations
80: - **jsPDF** for PDF reports
81: - **TypeScript** strict mode throughout
82: - RESTful API routes under `/api/*`
83: - Full CRUD on every resource
84: - Auto-migrations on first run (creates 35 tables)
85: 
86: ## Database schema (35 tables)
87: 
88: `users, workspaces, settings, contacts, companies, deals, activities, campaigns, content, emails, landing_pages, seo_keywords, seo_audits, ads, assets, automations, integrations, api_keys, copy_templates, notes, tasks, brand_assets, personas, segments, forms, form_submissions, lead_magnets, funnels, experiments, testimonials, events, event_registrations, competitors, conversations, budget_items, playbooks, surveys, survey_responses`
89: 
90: ## File map
91: 
92: ```
93: src/
94: ├── app/                              # 35 page routes
95: │   ├── page.tsx                      # Dashboard with module grid

metadata.preview:
# Marketing Hub — All-in-One Marketing Command Center

> ⚡ **Core Doctrine: Intensity Beats Extensity — Every Time.**
> One channel mastered beats five channels touched. One offer sharpened beats ten offers scattered. One persona deeply understood beats five personas guessed. Read the full principle in [`docs/INTENSITY_PRINCIPLE.md`](docs/INTENSITY_PRINCIPLE.md).

A complete marketing platform built with **Next.js 14, TypeScript, Tailwind, and SQLite**. **30 interconnected sections** covering the entire marketing workflow — all running locally, no external API keys required.

## Quick start

```bash
cd ~/marketing-hub
npm install
npm run seed               # populate with 200+ sample records
npm run dev                # http://localhost:3000
```

For production:
```bash
npm run build
npm start

<content>
1: # Digital Marketing Fundamentals
2: 
3: ## Definition
4: 
5: Digital marketing is the promotion of products, services, or brands through
6: internet-connected channels and technologies — search engines, social media,
7: email, websites, and mobile apps. The American Marketing Association defines it
8: broadly as "any marketing methods conducted through electronic devices which
9: utilize some form of a computer."
10: 
11: The defining difference from traditional marketing is **measurability**: nearly
12: every click, open, and purchase is trackable, turning marketing from guesswork
13: into a discipline.
14: 
15: ## The durable fundamentals (they outlast tactics)
16: 
17: 1. **Core channels** — SEO, content, social, email, paid ads, affiliate/influencer.
18: 2. **The owned / earned / paid media model.**
19: 3. **A data & measurement loop.**
20: 4. **The customer funnel applied across digital touchpoints.**
21: 
22: Tools and platforms change; these foundations don't.
23: 
24: ## The core channels
25: 
26: | Channel | What it does | Character |
27: | --- | --- | --- |
28: | SEO | Earn organic visibility when people search | Durable, high-trust, compounds (3–6+ mo) |
29: | Content marketing | Attract by being useful, not interruptive | Compounds; fuels other channels |
30: | Social media | Build audience, community, brand | Discovery + relationship |
31: | Email | Nurture an owned audience directly | Highest ROI (~$36–42 per $1) |
32: | Paid ads / PPC | Buy attention & traffic | Fast, controllable; stops with budget |
33: | Affiliate / influencer | Reach via partners' borrowed trust | Leverage others' audiences |
34: 
35: ## Owned, Earned, Paid media
36: 
37: - **Owned** — your website, blog, email list, app. You control them; they compound.
38: - **Earned** — press, shares, reviews, word of mouth. Can't buy directly; most credible.
39: - **Paid** — ads across search/social/display. Fast and controllable but rented.
40: 
41: A strong strategy balances all three: paid rents demand, owned builds assets,
42: earned grants credibility.
43: 
44: ## The data & measurement loop
45: 
46: - Visibility into what works lets you find and fix drop-off points.
47: - Modern principle: **"measure what matters, respect the customer, act on the
48:   data you can ethically collect"** — not "track everything" (privacy changes,
49:   cookie restrictions, regulation).
50: - Metrics that matter: CAC (customer acquisition cost), ROAS (return on ad
51:   spend), CPL (cost per lead), CLV/LTV (lifetime value), conversion rate.
52: - ROI formula: `(Revenue - Marketing Cost) / Marketing Cost × 100`.
53: - Rule of thumb benchmarks cited: aim for ~3:1 ROAS on paid, ~5:1 on organic.
54: 
55: ## A starter framework
56: 
57: | Step | Focus | Why |
58: | --- | --- | --- |
59: | 1 | Fundamentals | Understand channels, media model, data, funnel |
60: | 2 | One channel | Build real competence in a single discipline |
61: | 3 | A real project | Apply with actual stakes and feedback |
62: | 4 | Analytics | Set up measurement to learn what works |
63: | 5 | Integration | Add channels and connect them into one system |
64: 
65: ## 8-step strategy framework
66: 
67: 1. Define business objectives & SMART marketing goals.
68: 2. Understand your audience deeply.
69: 3. Audit your current digital presence.
70: 4. Select your channel mix.
71: 5. Develop your content strategy.
72: 6. Set budget and allocate resources.
73: 7. Implement tracking & attribution.
74: 8. Launch, measure, and optimize.
75: 
76: ## What's changing in 2026 (AI era)
77: 
78: - Discovery is going **visual and conversational**: Google AI Overviews (~16% of
79:   queries), ChatGPT search, Perplexity, Gemini, and platform-native search
80:   (TikTok, YouTube, Reddit).
81: - New optimization layers: **AEO** (Answer Engine Optimization — get cited by AI
82:   engines) and **GEO** (Generative Engine Optimization — content shaped for AI
83:   synthesis).
84: - Privacy-first marketing and first-party data ownership are now essential.
85: - The fundamentals stay the same; execution becomes faster, more personalized,
86:   more visual.
87: 
88: ## Typical result timelines
89: 
90: - Paid (PPC/social ads): leads within 24–48 hours.
91: - Social organic: engagement in days, conversion tuning 2–4 weeks.
92: - SEO: meaningful movement in 3–6 months, big gains 6–12 months.
93: - Email (existing list): immediate.
94: 
95: ## Sources
96: 
97: - What is digital marketing? — Adobe: https://business.adobe.com/blog/basics/digital-marketing
98: - Digital Marketing Complete Guide (2026) — PxlPeak: https://pxlpeak.com/guide/digital-marketing-complete-guide
99: - Fundamentals of Digital Marketing (2026) — BrandClickX: https://brandclickx.com/fundamentals-of-digital-marketing/
100: - Digital Marketing: The Complete 2026 Guide — Frostbite: https://frostbitemarketing.com/resources/guides/digital-marketing-guide-2026/
101: - What Is Digital Marketing? A Complete 2026 Guide — Three Girls Media: https://threegirlsmedia.co.uk/digital-marketing-explained-a-complete-2026-guide/
102: - Ultimate Guide to Digital Marketing (PDF) — DigitalMarketer: https://www.digitalmarketer.com/digital-marketing/assets/pdf/ultimate-guide-to-digital-marketing.pdf

(End of file - total 102 lines)
</content>

# Digital Marketing Fundamentals

## Definition

Digital marketing is the promotion of products, services, or brands through
internet-connected channels and technologies — search engines, social media,
email, websites, and mobile apps. The American Marketing Association defines it
broadly as "any marketing methods conducted through electronic devices which
utilize some form of a computer."

The defining difference from traditional marketing is **measurability**: nearly
every click, open, and purchase is trackable, turning marketing from guesswork
into a discipline.

## The durable fundamentals (they outlast tactics)

1. **Core channels** — SEO, content, social, email, paid ads, affiliate/influencer.
2. **The owned / earned / paid media model.**
3. **A data & measurement loop.**
4. **The customer funnel applied across digital touchpoints.**

=== 02-seo.md ===
# SEO (Search Engine Optimization)

Google-first reference, based on Google Search Central documentation plus current
2026 guidance (AEO/GEO for AI search).

## What SEO is

The practice of improving your website's content, structure, and authority so it
ranks higher in **unpaid (organic) search results**. One of the highest-ROI
channels because traffic is "free" once rankings are achieved, and it compounds.

## How Google Search works (crawl → index → serve)

- **Googlebot** discovers URLs by crawling links, sitemaps, and redirects.
- Pages are **indexed** (added to the index) if reachable and not blocked.
- Google **serves** relevant results to queries.

Key implication: if Googlebot can't find or understand a page, it can't rank.

## Essentials (Google Search Essentials)

- Create **helpful, reliable, people-first content**.
- Use words people actually search for, and place them naturally on the page.
- Make every page crawlable and indexable.
- Avoid deceptive or manipulative practices (cloaking, hidden text, link schemes).

## Beginner checklist (SEO Starter Guide)

1. **Descriptive page titles & meta descriptions** — unique per page; help users
   see relevance in results and can lift click-through.
2. **Use words people search for** in headings and body copy.
3. **Clear, logical site structure** — link important URLs together with
   descriptive anchor text.
4. **Images**: use descriptive filenames and `alt` text.
5. **Links**: build genuine, relevant links; use `rel="nofollow"` for paid/
   untrusted/required-login links.
6. **Duplicate content**: consolidate with canonical tags; pick one canonical URL.
7. **Mobile-friendly & fast**: Google uses a mobile crawler by default. Core Web
   Vitals matter.
8. **Secure (HTTPS)**: preferred and a ranking/trust signal.

=== 03-email-marketing.md ===
# Email Marketing

Focus: deliverability and compliance (getting to the inbox), list health, and
best practices. Email remains one of the highest-ROI channels (~$36–42 per $1
spent).

## Why deliverability matters

Great content is worthless if it lands in spam. Inbox placement depends on
**sender reputation**, authentication, list quality, sending behavior, and
engagement signals.

## Authentication (mandatory)

Set up all three DNS-based standards:

- **SPF (Sender Policy Framework)** — authorizes which servers may send for your
  domain. Keep the record updated; don't exceed **10 DNS lookups** (SPF auto-fails).
- **DKIM (DomainKeys Identified Mail)** — cryptographic signature proving the
  domain owner sent the message. Use 1024- or 2048-bit keys.
- **DMARC (Domain-Based Message Authentication, Reporting & Conformance)** —
  tells receivers what to do on auth failure. Start at `p=none` (monitor), then
  move to `p=quarantine` or `p=reject`. Requires **domain alignment**: the `From`
  domain must match the one passing SPF or DKIM.
- **BIMI (Brand Indicators for Message Identification)** — shows your logo in
  inbox; requires DMARC `p=quarantine`/`reject` + a VMC (Verified Mark Certificate).

Gmail requirement (Feb 1, 2024): senders of **>5,000 messages/day** to Gmail must
have SPF + DKIM + DMARC, valid forward/reverse DNS (PTR), TLS, and **one-click
unsubscribe**.

## Sender reputation & monitoring

- Use **dedicated vs shared IP** intentionally; shared IPs tie your fate to others.
- Monitor with Google Postmaster Tools, Sender Score, and your ESP dashboards.
- Keep **spam rate below 0.10%**, never reaching 0.30% (Gmail/Yahoo guidance).
- A single spam-trap hit can take 6–12 months to recover from.

## List health (permission-first)


=== 04-social-media-marketing.md ===
# Social Media Marketing

SMM = using social platforms to connect with your audience to build brand,
increase sales, and drive traffic — via publishing, engagement, listening, and
paid social ads.

## The #1 principle: focus, don't spray

- **Pick 1–2 primary platforms** where your audience actually spends time. A
  dominant presence on 2 beats a mediocre presence on 5–6.
- Random acts of content lead to random results. Strategy first.
- Use the **"rule of two"**: one **discovery** platform (TikTok, Reels, Shorts,
  Pinterest) + one **relationship** platform (LinkedIn, Threads, Reddit, email).

## The 7-step strategy cycle (Buffer)

1. Set **SMART goals**.
2. Define your **target audience**.
3. Choose **content pillars** (repeatable themes).
4. Choose your **platforms** (where audience is).
5. Build a **content calendar**.
6. **Analyze** performance.
7. **Evaluate** and iterate (it's a flywheel, not a one-time plan).

## Content pillars & ratio

- Most successful brands use a **70/30 value-to-promo** split (or 60/25/15:
  60% educational, 25% personal/brand, 15% promotional).
- A working pillar set = 4–6 pillars, each with a fixed output ratio, format, and
  owner. Example: 40% educate, 30% entertain, 20% inspire, 10% promote.
- **UGC (user-generated content)** can see ~8.7x higher engagement than
  brand-produced content in some verticals — audiences trust peers.

## Platform notes (2026 benchmarks)

- **TikTok** — best organic reach (~5.3% engagement). Vertical 9:16, hook in
  first 2 sec, trending audio, authenticity over polish. Strong for under-35 B2C.
- **LinkedIn** — ~6.5% organic engagement; **personal posts get ~561% more reach
  than company pages**. Best for B2B. Long-form text, document carousels, native
  video, thoughtful comments.

=== 05-paid-ads-ppc.md ===
# Paid Ads / PPC (Pay-Per-Click)

Primary focus: **Google Ads** campaign structure & best practices, plus general
PPC principles. PPC buys targeted traffic fast; it stops when the budget stops,
so it pairs with (doesn't replace) organic.

## What PPC is

You pay per click (or impression/action) to show ads on search engines, social
platforms, and display networks. Highest-intent traffic: people searching for
what you offer. Google Search Ads cited at ~4.4% avg conversion rate; organic
converts ~14.6% vs 1.7% for outbound.

## Google Ads account structure

Top-down hierarchy:

```
Account
└── Campaign        (budget, location, network, settings)
    └── Ad Group    (theme + related keywords + ads)
        └── Ads + Keywords
```

**Best practices**

- Organize as you create; reorganizing later wipes accumulated data/learning.
- Create a **separate campaign** when you need different budget or location
  targeting (e.g., shirts vs pants).
- One campaign can share budget/location across ad groups.
- Each **ad group = one narrow theme** with tightly related keywords.
- Mention at least one keyword in the ad headline for relevance.
- Keep **≥3 ads per ad group** and use optimized ad rotation to let Google pick
  the best per auction (signals: keyword, search term, device, location).

## Campaign types

Choose by objective:

- **Search** — text ads on search results (high intent).

=== 06-content-marketing.md ===
# Content Marketing

Content marketing attracts, engages, and converts audiences through genuinely
useful content (articles, guides, video, infographics) rather than interruptive
ads. It feeds SEO, social, and email, and compounds over time.

## Definition

A **documented plan** defining what content you'll create, for whom, through which
channels, and how you'll measure success — with every piece tied to a business
goal.

## The 6-component framework (CMI / 2026 synthesis)

1. **Strategic foundation** — goals/objectives (awareness, demand gen, pipeline,
   retention).
2. **Audience intelligence** — for B2B, map the full **buying committee** (a VP
   and a CTO have different needs); refresh personas annually.
3. **Content architecture** — 3–5 **content pillars** (buyer pain areas, not
   product categories) + topic clusters.
4. **Production & editorial process** — editorial calendar (themes over time) +
   content calendar (per piece: keyword, persona, funnel stage, owner, date).
5. **Distribution & promotion** — plan this *before* writing. Budget 25–35% of
   content spend on distribution for mature B2B.
6. **Measurement & analytics** — KPIs before copy.

> Only **47% of B2B marketers have a documented strategy** (CMI, 2025). Only ~3%
> of your market is buying now; build for the 97% still researching.

## Build it in 9 steps

1. Define goals.
2. Research audience (CRM data, sales calls, social analytics; map buying committee).
3. Run a **content audit** (inventory; tag keep/update/consolidate/remove).
4. Choose a CMS + governance model (who can publish, review, style).
5. Determine content types & formats.
6. Map content to the buyer journey (TOFU/MOFU/BOFU).
7. Build the distribution engine (plan "where will this live?" first).
8. Add the **AEO layer** (see below).
9. Implement measurement from day one.

  domain. Keep the record updated; don't exceed **10 DNS lookups** (SPF auto-fails).
- **DKIM (DomainKeys Identified Mail)** — cryptographic signature proving the
  domain owner sent the message. Use 1024- or 2048-bit keys.
- **DMARC (Domain-Based Message Authentication, Reporting & Conformance)** —
  tells receivers what to do on auth failure. Start at `p=none` (monitor), then
  move to `p=quarantine` or `p=reject`. Requires **domain alignment**: the `From`
  domain must match the one passing SPF or DKIM.
- **BIMI (Brand Indicators for Message Identification)** — shows your logo in
  inbox; requires DMARC `p=quarantine`/`reject` + a VMC (Verified Mark Certificate).

Gmail requirement (Feb 1, 2024): senders of **>5,000 messages/day** to Gmail must
have SPF + DKIM + DMARC, valid forward/reverse DNS (PTR), TLS, and **one-click
unsubscribe**.

## Sender reputation & monitoring

- Use **dedicated vs shared IP** intentionally; shared IPs tie your fate to others.
- Monitor with Google Postmaster Tools, Sender Score, and your ESP dashboards.
- Keep **spam rate below 0.10%**, never reaching 0.30% (Gmail/Yahoo guidance).
- A single spam-trap hit can take 6–12 months to recover from.

## List health (permission-first)


=== 04-social-media-marketing.md ===
# Social Media Marketing

SMM = using social platforms to connect with your audience to build brand,
increase sales, and drive traffic — via publishing, engagement, listening, and
paid social ads.

## The #1 principle: focus, don't spray

- **Pick 1–2 primary platforms** where your audience actually spends time. A
  dominant presence on 2 beats a mediocre presence on 5–6.
- Random acts of content lead to random results. Strategy first.
- Use the **"rule of two"**: one **discovery** platform (TikTok, Reels, Shorts,
  Pinterest) + one **relationship** platform (LinkedIn, Threads, Reddit, email).

## The 7-step strategy cycle (Buffer)

1. Set **SMART goals**.
2. Define your **target audience**.
3. Choose **content pillars** (repeatable themes).
4. Choose your **platforms** (where audience is).
5. Build a **content calendar**.
6. **Analyze** performance.
7. **Evaluate** and iterate (it's a flywheel, not a one-time plan).

## Content pillars & ratio

- Most successful brands use a **70/30 value-to-promo** split (or 60/25/15:
  60% educational, 25% personal/brand, 15% promotional).
- A working pillar set = 4–6 pillars, each with a fixed output ratio, format, and
  owner. Example: 40% educate, 30% entertain, 20% inspire, 10% promote.
- **UGC (user-generated content)** can see ~8.7x higher engagement than
  brand-produced content in some verticals — audiences trust peers.

## Platform notes (2026 benchmarks)

- **TikTok** — best organic reach (~5.3% engagement). Vertical 9:16, hook in
  first 2 sec, trending audio, authenticity over polish. Strong for under-35 B2C.
- **LinkedIn** — ~6.5% organic engagement; **personal posts get ~561% more reach
  than company pages**. Best for B2B. Long-form text, document carousels, native
  video, thoughtful comments.

=== 05-paid-ads-ppc.md ===
# Paid Ads / PPC (Pay-Per-Click)

Primary focus: **Google Ads** campaign structure & best practices, plus general
PPC principles. PPC buys targeted traffic fast; it stops when the budget stops,
so it pairs with (doesn't replace) organic.

## What PPC is

You pay per click (or impression/action) to show ads on search engines, social
platforms, and display networks. Highest-intent traffic: people searching for
what you offer. Google Search Ads cited at ~4.4% avg conversion rate; organic
converts ~14.6% vs 1.7% for outbound.

## Google Ads account structure

Top-down hierarchy:

```
Account
└── Campaign        (budget, location, network, settings)
    └── Ad Group    (theme + related keywords + ads)
        └── Ads + Keywords
```

**Best practices**

- Organize as you create; reorganizing later wipes accumulated data/learning.
- Create a **separate campaign** when you need different budget or location
  targeting (e.g., shirts vs pants).
- One campaign can share budget/location across ad groups.
- Each **ad group = one narrow theme** with tightly related keywords.
- Mention at least one keyword in the ad headline for relevance.
- Keep **≥3 ads per ad group** and use optimized ad rotation to let Google pick
  the best per auction (signals: keyword, search term, device, location).

## Campaign types

Choose by objective:

- **Search** — text ads on search results (high intent).

=== 06-content-marketing.md ===
# Content Marketing

Content marketing attracts, engages, and converts audiences through genuinely
useful content (articles, guides, video, infographics) rather than interruptive
ads. It feeds SEO, social, and email, and compounds over time.

## Definition

A **documented plan** defining what content you'll create, for whom, through which
channels, and how you'll measure success — with every piece tied to a business
goal.

## The 6-component framework (CMI / 2026 synthesis)

1. **Strategic foundation** — goals/objectives (awareness, demand gen, pipeline,
   retention).
2. **Audience intelligence** — for B2B, map the full **buying committee** (a VP
   and a CTO have different needs); refresh personas annually.
3. **Content architecture** — 3–5 **content pillars** (buyer pain areas, not
   product categories) + topic clusters.
4. **Production & editorial process** — editorial calendar (themes over time) +
   content calendar (per piece: keyword, persona, funnel stage, owner, date).
5. **Distribution & promotion** — plan this *before* writing. Budget 25–35% of
   content spend on distribution for mature B2B.
6. **Measurement & analytics** — KPIs before copy.

> Only **47% of B2B marketers have a documented strategy** (CMI, 2025). Only ~3%
> of your market is buying now; build for the 97% still researching.

## Build it in 9 steps

1. Define goals.
2. Research audience (CRM data, sales calls, social analytics; map buying committee).
3. Run a **content audit** (inventory; tag keep/update/consolidate/remove).
4. Choose a CMS + governance model (who can publish, review, style).
5. Determine content types & formats.
6. Map content to the buyer journey (TOFU/MOFU/BOFU).
7. Build the distribution engine (plan "where will this live?" first).
8. Add the **AEO layer** (see below).
9. Implement measurement from day one.

data.text:
Now I have a comprehensive understanding. The marketing hub is a Next.js app with a `/marketing` section containing tabs (Dashboard, Ads Audit, AI Skills, Campaigns, Persona). I need to add a "Tests" / "Exams" feature — marketing exams for professionals, suited for recruiting based on skill.

Let me design this:
- A new tab "Tests" (or "Recruiting") in the marketing hub page
- A new component `MarketingTests.tsx` 
- A data file with multiple marketing exams across disciplines: fundamentals, SEO, email, social, paid ads, content
- An exam runner: choose a test, take it (multiple choice + scenario), get scored, see skill breakdown
- Recruiting angle: scoring maps to skill levels (Junior / Mid / Senior / Expert), per-discipline scoring
- Results can be exported

Let me build:
1. `src/lib/marketing-tests/exams.ts` — exam question bank (big, real, professional questions)
2. `src/components/marketing/MarketingTests.tsx` — the UI with test picker, exam runner, results
3. Update `src/app/marketing/page.tsx` to add the Tests tab
4. Optionally an API route to score results server-side — but I can do scoring client-side to keep it self-contained. Actually, to be consistent with the existing pattern (skills API), I could add `/api/marketing/tests` route. But client-side scoring is fine for an exam. Let me keep it self-contained in the component for simplicity and not require server round-trips.

Let me design exam structure. Each exam covers a marketing discipline. Each exam has questions with multiple choices, an answer key, difficulty, skill tag, and explanation. Scenario-based questions for senior-level assessment.

Exams:
1. Digital Marketing Fundamentals
2. SEO
3. Email Marketing
4. Social Media Marketing
5. Paid Ads / PPC
6. Content Marketing
7. Marketing Strategy & Analytics (mixed senior-level)

Each exam ~15-20 questions. Recruiting: a candidate can take one or all; results show score %, grade, skill level, per-domain breakdown, and time taken.

Let me write the question bank based on the marketing-docs content (which is accurate) plus standard professional marketing knowledge. I'll make questions genuinely testing, with explanations.

Given size, I'll create a well-structured data file. Let me make it substantial but not absurdly huge — maybe 6 exams × ~12 questions = ~72 questions, each with 4 options, correct index, difficulty, skill tag, explanation. That's a solid professional test bank.

Let me start building. I'll use the existing `ui.tsx` Card, Button, etc.

Let me write the exams data file first.

I have enough context. I'll build a marketing exams/tests feature in the marketing hub — a question bank covering 6 marketing disciplines, with a recruiting-grade scoring system (skill level per domain, grade, pass/fail, explanations). Let me create the data file, the component, wire the tab, and verify the build.

data.text: I have enough context. Let me create the exams data file with a comprehensive question bank across 6 marketing domains.

input.content:
export type QuestionType = "mcq" | "truefalse";
export type Difficulty = 1 | 2 | 3;

export interface Question {
  id: string;
  type: QuestionType;
  prompt: string;
  options: string[];
  correct: number;
  explanation: string;
  skill: string;
  difficulty: Difficulty;
}

export interface Exam {
  id: string;
  title: string;
  domain: string;
  description: string;
  durationMinutes: number;
  passingScore: number;
  level: "associate" | "professional" | "expert";
  questions: Question[];
}

export interface SkillBand {
  min: number;
  max: number;
  band: string;
  label: string;
}

export const SKILL_BANDS: SkillBand[] = [
  { min: 0, max: 49, band: "F", label: "Junior — fundamentals missing" },
  { min: 50, max: 64, band: "D", label: "Junior — partial grasp" },
  { min: 65, max: 74, band: "C", label: "Mid — working knowledge" },
  { min: 75, max: 84, band: "B", label: "Senior — solid practitioner" },
  { min: 85, max: 92, band: "A", label: "Lead — strong command" },
  { min: 93, max: 100, band: "A+", label: "Expert — recruiting-grade mastery" },
];

export function bandFor(score: number): SkillBand {
  return SKILL_BANDS.find((b) => score >= b.min && score <= b.max) ?? SKILL_BANDS[0]!;
}

export const EXAMS: Exam[] = [
  {
    id: "fundamentals",
    title: "Digital Marketing Fundamentals",
    domain: "Fundamentals",
    description:
      "Core channels, owned/earned/paid media, the measurement loop, and the customer funnel. Foundational knowledge every professional must own.",
    durationMinutes: 12,
    passingScore: 70,
    level: "associate",
    questions: [
      {
        id: "f1",
        type: "mcq",
        prompt:
          "Which best describes the defining difference between digital and traditional marketing?",
        options: [
          "Digital marketing only runs on social media platforms.",
          "Digital marketing is measurable at nearly every touchpoint (clicks, opens, purchases).",
          "Digital marketing is always cheaper than traditional marketing.",
          "Digital marketing cannot use television or radio channels.",
        ],
        correct: 1,
        explanation:
          "The defining difference is measurability — nearly every interaction is trackable, turning marketing from guesswork into a data discipline.",
        skill: "concepts",
        difficulty: 1,
      },
      {
        id: "f2",
        type: "mcq",
        prompt: "Which channel pair is correctly characterized?",
        options: [
          "SEO — fast, controllable, stops with budget.",
          "Paid ads — durable, compounds over 3–6 months.",
          "Email — highest reported ROI (~$36–42 per $1 spent).",
          "Content marketing — fastest channel for leads (24–48h).",
        ],
        correct: 2,
        explanation:
          "Email is repeatedly cited as the highest-ROI owned channel (~$36–42 per $1). Paid ads are fast/controllable, SEO and content compound slowly.",
        skill: "channels",
        difficulty: 2,
      },
      {
        id: "f3",
        type: "mcq",
        prompt:
          "Your company blog, email list, and website are best classified as:",
        options: ["Paid media", "Earned media", "Owned media", "Shared media"],
        correct: 2,
        explanation:
          "Owned media = assets you control (website, blog, email list, app). They compound and you don't rent them.",
        skill: "media-model",
        difficulty: 1,
      },
      {
        id: "f4",
        type: "mcq",
        prompt:
          "A campaign spent $5,000 and generated $18,000 in attributed revenue. What is the ROI?",
        options: ["260%", "360%", "72%", "180%"],
        correct: 0,
        explanation:
          "ROI = (Revenue - Cost) / Cost × 100 = (18000 - 5000) / 5000 × 100 = 260%.",
        skill: "measurement",
        difficulty: 2,
      },
      {
        id: "f5",
        type: "mcq",
        prompt: "Which is the recommended rule-of-thumb ROAS benchmark?",
        options: [
          "~1:1 on paid, ~10:1 on organic.",
          "~3:1 on paid, ~5:1 on organic.",
          "~10:1 on paid, ~3:1 on organic.",
          "ROAS is not a useful metric.",
        ],
        correct: 1,
        explanation:
          "Common cited benchmarks: ~3:1 ROAS on paid, ~5:1 on organic. A strong strategy balances paid, owned, and earned.",
        skill: "measurement",
        difficulty: 1,
      },
      {
        id: "f6",
        type: "mcq",
        prompt:
          "Which sequence is the correct starter framework for building a digital marketing capability?",
        options: [
          "Pick tools → hire team → run ads → measure → hope.",
          "Fundamentals → one channel → a real project → analytics → integration.",
          "Launch everywhere → analyze → cut losers → scale winners.",
          "Buy data → build funnel → run paid → optimize → exit.",
        ],
        correct: 1,
        explanation:
          "The durable starter path: master fundamentals, build competence in ONE channel, apply on a real project with stakes, set up analytics, then integrate channels into one system.",
        skill: "strategy",
        difficulty: 2,
      },
      {
        id: "f7",
        type: "truefalse",
        prompt:
          "A 70/30 value-to-promo content ratio is a widely-cited healthy balance for building audience while still converting.",
        options: ["True", "False"],

          "Plan distribution first ('where will this live?'), then write, then publish.",
          "Publish first, decide audience later.",
          "Distribute only after the asset underperforms organically.",
        ],
        correct: 1,
        explanation:
          "Plan distribution before writing. Knowing where the asset lives and how it gets distributed shapes format and messaging.",
        skill: "distribution",
        difficulty: 2,
      },
    ],
  },
];

export function getExam(id: string): Exam | undefined {
  return EXAMS.find((e) => e.id === id);
}

+          "Paid / Earned / Owned.",
+          "TOFU / MOFU / BOFU only.",
+        ],
+        correct: 1,
+        explanation:
+          "Audit inventory and tag each asset: keep, update, consolidate, or remove. This reveals gaps and avoids duplication.",
+        skill: "process",
+        difficulty: 2,
+      },
+      {
+        id: "c7",
+        type: "truefalse",
+        prompt:
+          "Only ~3% of your market is actively buying now; content should also serve the ~97% still researching.",
+        options: ["True", "False"],
+        correct: 0,
+        explanation:
+          "Building for the 97% in research mode compounds demand so that when they're ready to buy, you're the obvious choice.",
+        skill: "strategy",
+        difficulty: 2,
+      },
+      {
+        id: "c8",
+        type: "mcq",
+        prompt: "The correct sequencing for a new content asset is:",
+        options: [
+          "Write → Publish → Hope → Distribute.",
+          "Plan distribution first ('where will this live?'), then write, then publish.",
+          "Publish first, decide audience later.",
+          "Distribute only after the asset underperforms organically.",
+        ],
+        correct: 1,
+        explanation:
+          "Plan distribution before writing. Knowing where the asset lives and how it gets distributed shapes format and messaging.",
+        skill: "distribution",
+        difficulty: 2,
+      },
+    ],
+  },
+];
+
+export function getExam(id: string): Exam | undefined {
+  return EXAMS.find((e) => e.id === id);
+}

Index: /home/uzer/marketing-hub/src/lib/marketing-tests/exams.ts
===================================================================
--- /home/uzer/marketing-hub/src/lib/marketing-tests/exams.ts
+++ /home/uzer/marketing-hub/src/lib/marketing-tests/exams.ts
@@ -0,0 +1,917 @@
+export type QuestionType = "mcq" | "truefalse";
+export type Difficulty = 1 | 2 | 3;
+
+export interface Question {
+  id: string;
+  type: QuestionType;
+  prompt: string;
+  options: string[];
+  correct: number;
+  explanation: string;
+  skill: string;
+  difficulty: Difficulty;
+}
+
+export interface Exam {
+  id: string;
+  title: string;
+  domain: string;
+  description: string;
+  durationMinutes: number;
+  passingScore: number;
+  level: "associate" | "professional" | "expert";
+  questions: Question[];
+}
+
+export interface SkillBand {
+  min: number;
+  max: number;
+  band: string;
+  label: string;
+}
+
+export const SKILL_BANDS: SkillBand[] = [
+  { min: 0, max: 49, band: "F", label: "Junior — fundamentals missing" },
+  { min: 50, max: 64, band: "D", label: "Junior — partial grasp" },
+  { min: 65, max: 74, band: "C", label: "Mid — working knowledge" },
+  { min: 75, max: 84, band: "B", label: "Senior — solid practitioner" },
+  { min: 85, max: 92, band: "A", label: "Lead — strong command" },
+  { min: 93, max: 100, band: "A+", label: "Expert — recruiting-grade mastery" },
+];
+
+export function bandFor(score: number): SkillBand {
+  return SKILL_BANDS.find((b) => score >= b.min && score <= b.max) ?? SKILL_BANDS[0]!;
+}
+
+export const EXAMS: Exam[] = [
+  {

+    passingScore: 70,

+        id: "f1",

+        options: [
+          "Digital marketing only runs on social media platforms.",
+          "Digital marketing is measurable at nearly every touchpoint (clicks, opens, purchases).",
+          "Digital marketing is always cheaper than traditional marketing.",
+          "Digital marketing cannot use television or radio channels.",
+        ],
+        correct: 1,
+        explanation:
+          "The defining difference is measurability — nearly every interaction is trackable, turning marketing from guesswork into a data discipline.",
+        skill: "concepts",
+        difficulty: 1,
+      },
+      {
+        id: "f2",
+        type: "mcq",
+        prompt: "Which channel pair is correctly characterized?",
+        options: [
+          "SEO — fast, controllable, stops with budget.",
+          "Paid ads — durable, compounds over 3–6 months.",
+          "Email — highest reported ROI (~$36–42 per $1 spent).",
+          "Content marketing — fastest channel for leads (24–48h).",
+        ],
+        correct: 2,
+        explanation:
+          "Email is repeatedly cited as the highest-ROI owned channel (~$36–42 per $1). Paid ads are fast/controllable, SEO and content compound slowly.",
+        skill: "channels",
+        difficulty: 2,
+      },
+      {
+        id: "f3",
+        type: "mcq",
+        prompt:
+          "Your company blog, email list, and website are best classified as:",
+        options: ["Paid media", "Earned media", "Owned media", "Shared media"],
+        correct: 2,
+        explanation:
+          "Owned media = assets you control (website, blog, email list, app). They compound and you don't rent them.",
+        skill: "media-model",
+        difficulty: 1,
+      },
+      {
+        id: "f4",
+        type: "mcq",
+        prompt:
+          "A campaign spent $5,000 and generated $18,000 in attributed revenue. What is the ROI?",
+        options: ["260%", "360%", "72%", "180%"],
+        correct: 0,
+        explanation:
+          "ROI = (Revenue - Cost) / Cost × 100 = (18000 - 5000) / 5000 × 100 = 260%.",
+        skill: "measurement",
+        difficulty: 2,

+      {
+        id: "f5",

+        prompt:

data.text:
Added a recruiting-grade **Marketing Tests** feature to the Marketing Hub at `/marketing` → **Tests** tab.

## What was built

**`src/lib/marketing-tests/exams.ts`** — question bank + scoring engine:
- 6 professional exams (43 questions total): Fundamentals, SEO, Email/Deliverability, Social Media, Paid Ads/PPC, Content Marketing
- Each question is skill-tagged, difficulty-rated (1–3), MCQ or true/false, with a teaching explanation
- Sourced from the project's own `marketing-docs` corpus (benchmarks, formulas, Google/Gmail rules)
- 6-band recruiting grade scale (F → A+) with pass thresholds per exam

**`src/components/marketing/MarketingTests.tsx`** — exam runner with three phases:
- **Picker**: exam cards showing level, question count, duration, pass mark
- **Running**: shuffled questions, progress bar, skill/difficulty badges, question navigator, submit guard
- **Results**: band grade (A+/A/B/C/D/F), pass/fail, per-skill breakdown bars, full answer review with explanations, recruiter signal text, print/PDF report

**`src/app/marketing/page.tsx`** — added the "Tests" tab (now 6 tabs).

## Recruiting output
Each completed exam produces a report a hiring manager can act on: overall band + pass/fail, per-skill percentages (e.g. `authentication 88%`, `list-health 50%`), and an explicit recruiter signal ("Strong hire", "Pass — probe weaker skills", "Below threshold").

## Verification
- `tsc --noEmit` against the full project: **zero errors** in any of the three new/changed files (confirmed by filtering).
- `next build` fails on a **pre-existing** webpack error in `src/lib/smarketing.ts` (`avgDealSize` redeclaration) — unrelated to this work and present in the working tree before my edits. `next lint` is broken project-wide by an ESLint v10 incompatibility (also pre-existing).
- No new dependencies introduced; uses only the existing `@/components/marketing/ui` primitives (Card, Button, Badge) and stays within the established patterns of `SkillsPanel.tsx` / `PersonaBuilder.tsx`.

data.text:
The user wants to expand the Marketing Tests. Let me think about what would meaningfully expand it as a recruiting-grade assessment:

1. Scenario/case-study questions — longer-form situational judgment
2. Timer — per-exam countdown with auto-submit
3. Candidate name capture — already have placeholder in code but no UI field
4. Persistent results history — save attempts to localStorage (or DB) and show history

Let me propose a concrete plan. But the user said "plan/code" — so I should plan then code. Let me lay out a concise plan and then implement. I'll present the plan and proceed without further confirmation since the user wants me to act.

Let me plan the expansion:

1. **Candidate identity capture** — add a name/email field on the picker before starting (currently "Anonymous Candidate").
2. **Exam timer** — countdown from durationMinutes, auto-submit on expiry, visible countdown.
3. **Scenario questions** — add a new exam type with case-study/scenario questions (multi-select? or longer MCQ with context). To keep scoring deterministic, scenario = MCQ but with a longer context stem. Add 1-2 new scenario-based exams.
4. **Results history** — persist attempts in localStorage with key, show a "History" panel with recent attempts, allow re-viewing a past report.
5. **More questions / a new exam domain** — add a 7th exam (e.g. Marketing Analytics & Measurement, or Brand/Strategy) to broaden coverage.

706:         difficulty: 2,

729:         skill: "metrics",
730:         difficulty: 1,
731:       },
732:       {

735:         prompt: "Which campaign type best matches high purchase intent?",
736:         options: [

739:           "Awareness video ads.",
740:           "Companion banner ads.",
741:         ],
742:         correct: 1,
743:         explanation:
744:           "Search captures people actively querying — highest intent. Display/video are better for awareness/retargeting.",
745:         skill: "campaign-types",
746:         difficulty: 1,
747:       },
748:       {
749:         id: "p9",
750:         type: "mcq",
751:         prompt:
752:           "Optimized ad rotation lets Google pick the best ad per auction based on signals including:",

755:           "Only the advertiser's favorite ad.",
756:           "The alphabetically first ad.",

state.title: marketing-hub/src/lib/marketing-tests/exams.ts

data.text: Now let me add the 7th scenario-based exam (Marketing Analytics & Attribution) before the closing `];`.

input.newString:
{
    id: "analytics",
    title: "Marketing Analytics & Attribution (Scenario Exam)",
    domain: "Analytics",
    description:
      "Situational, recruiting-grade questions on attribution models, funnel math, CAC/LTV, conversion-rate optimization, and data interpretation. Tests applied judgement, not just recall.",
    durationMinutes: 18,
    passingScore: 80,
    level: "expert",
    questions: [
      {
        id: "a1",
        type: "mcq",
        prompt:
          "Scenario: Last-click attribution credits the entire $4,000 sale to a branded search ad the user clicked 5 minutes before buying. But the user first discovered you via a YouTube review 3 weeks earlier, then read two blog posts, then opened an email. What is the main risk of optimizing purely on last-click?",
        options: [
          "You will overinvest in branded search and starve the upper-funnel channels that actually create demand.",
          "You will spend too little on branded search.",
          "Last-click is illegal under GDPR.",
          "There is no risk — last-click is the only correct model.",
        ],
        correct: 0,
        explanation:
          "Last-click over-credits bottom-funnel (branded search, direct) and under-credits the demand-generating upper funnel (content, social, video). Pure last-click optimization leads to a shrinking pipeline masked by stable conversion numbers.",
        skill: "attribution",
        difficulty: 3,
      },
      {
        id: "a2",
        type: "mcq",
        prompt:
          "Scenario: Your funnel shows Visitors → Leads → MQL → SQL → Customer with 100,000 → 5,000 → 1,200 → 400 → 80. Where is the biggest conversion leak by step rate?",
        options: [
          "Visitors → Leads (95% drop, 5% conv).",
          "Leads → MQL (76% drop, 24% conv).",
          "MQL → SQL (67% drop, 33% conv).",
          "SQL → Customer (80% drop, 20% conv).",
        ],
        correct: 0,
        explanation:
          "Visitors → Leads is the largest absolute and proportional drop (100k → 5k = 95% loss, 5% conversion). Fix top-of-funnel capture/landing quality first. The biggest 'rate' drop is SQL→Customer (80%), but it affects far fewer people, so prioritize by absolute loss × fixability.",
        skill: "funnel-math",
        difficulty: 3,
      },
      {
        id: "a3",
        type: "mcq",
        prompt:
          "Scenario: CAC is $200. Average customer pays $50/month with 80% gross margin and churns after an average of 18 months. Ignoring expansion, what is the LTV, and is the unit economics healthy (LTV:CAC ≥ 3:1)?",
        options: [
          "LTV = $720; LTV:CAC = 3.6:1 → healthy.",
          "LTV = $900; LTV:CAC = 4.5:1 → healthy.",
          "LTV = $720; LTV:CAC = 3.6:1 → unhealthy (below 5:1).",
          "LTV = $50; LTV:CAC = 0.25:1 → unhealthy.",
        ],
        correct: 0,
        explanation:
          "LTV = monthly revenue × gross margin × lifetime = $50 × 0.80 × 18 = $720. LTV:CAC = 720/200 = 3.6:1. The common healthy benchmark is ≥ 3:1, so this is healthy (not exceptional).",
        skill: "unit-economics",
        difficulty: 3,
      },
      {
        id: "a4",
        type: "mcq",
        prompt:
          "Scenario: An A/B test on a landing page reports Variant B lifts conversion 8% with 95% confidence. Variant A's baseline is 4.00%. Variant B tested on 10,000 visitors split 50/50. The stakeholder wants to ship B to all traffic immediately. What is the most defensible recommendation?",
        options: [
          "Ship B everywhere immediately — 95% confidence is conclusive.",
          "Verify the practical significance: an 8% relative lift on 4% = 4.32%, a +0.32pp absolute gain. Check the sample reached the pre-calculated MDE, segment for novelty effects, then ramp 50%→100% with guardrails.",
          "Reject B — only 10%+ lifts are worth shipping.",
          "Run the test for another year before deciding.",
        ],
        correct: 1,
        explanation:
          "Statistical significance ≠ practical significance. Confirm the effect size clears the pre-registered MDE, watch for novelty/segment skew, then ramp with guardrail metrics (bounce, revenue, support tickets) rather than flipping to 100% blindly.",
        skill: "experimentation",
        difficulty: 3,
      },
      {
        id: "a5",
        type: "mcq",
        prompt:
          "Scenario: A campaign generated 12,000 clicks and 360 conversions at a cost of $1,800. What are the CPC and CVR, and what's the first optimization question to ask?",
        options: [
          "CPC = $0.15, CVR = 3%. Ask: is the landing page matching the ad's promise?",
          "CPC = $1.50, CVR = 30%. Ask: should we double budget?",
          "CPC = $0.15, CVR = 30%. Ask: why is CVR so high?",
          "CPC = $5.00, CVR = 3%. Ask: should we kill the campaign?",
        ],
        correct: 0,
        explanation:
          "CPC = cost/clicks = 1800/12000 = $0.15. CVR = conversions/clicks = 360/12000 = 3%. Cheap clicks with a 3% CVR is reasonable; the next lever is usually message-match between ad and landing page.",
        skill: "metrics",
        difficulty: 2,
      },
      {
        id: "a6",
        type: "mcq",
        prompt:
          "Scenario: Your multi-touch attribution model shows Content gets 30% of conversion credit, Paid Search 25%, Email 25%, Direct 20%. Paid Search has a CPA of $40 and Content has a CPA of $15. The CFO wants to cut Content budget to fund more Paid Search. What's the analytical flaw?",
        options: [
          "There is no flaw — Paid Search has higher absolute credit (no, it doesn't).",
          "Content has a far better CPA ($15 vs $40) AND drives upper-funnel assist credit. Cutting it will likely raise blended CAC and starve Paid Search of assisted demand.",
          "Content should always be cut because it's free.",
          "Direct should be cut first because it has 0 cost.",
        ],
        correct: 1,
        explanation:
          "Content's lower CPA ($15) plus its assist role means cutting it typically raises blended CAC and reduces the assisted conversions Paid Search claims credit for. Optimize by marginal CPA and assist value, not absolute credit alone.",
        skill: "attribution",
        difficulty: 3,
      },
      {
        id: "a7",
        type: "mcq",
        prompt:
          "Scenario: You see organic traffic drop 40% overnight on a single date. What should you check FIRST, in order of likelihood?",
        options: [
          "Ignore it — Google updates traffic daily.",
          "Check: (1) GSC for a manual action or core update, (2) robots.txt/noindex accidental change, (3) a site outage or deploy on that date, (4) tracking/Analytics tag breakage.",
          "Immediately disavow all backlinks and rewrite all content.",
          "Switch to a new domain.",
        ],
        correct: 1,
        explanation:
          "Sudden step-changes are almost always: a core/manual update, a deploy that broke crawlability/indexing, a tracking break, or an outage. Diagnose before changing content or links.",
        skill: "diagnostics",
        difficulty: 3,
      },
      {
        id: "a8",
        type: "truefalse",
        prompt:
          "Scenario: Your blended CAC is $150, paid CAC is $300, organic CAC is $50. Statement: \"Paid acquisition is unprofitable and should be eliminated.\" Is this reasoning sound?",
        options: ["True — the reasoning is sound", "False — the reasoning is flawed"],
        correct: 1,
        explanation:
          "Flawed. Paid often has higher direct CAC but funds upper-funnel awareness that organic 'cheats' on. Judge paid on marginal CAC and its assist contribution, not direct-attributed CAC alone. Eliminating it can collapse organic demand.",
        skill: "unit-economics",
        difficulty: 3,
      },
      {
        id: "a9",
        type: "mcq",
        prompt:
          "Scenario: You're choosing a primary attribution model for a 90-day B2B SaaS funnel with multiple decision-makers. Which model is most defensible and why?",
        options: [
          "Last-click — simplest and accurate enough.",
          "First-click — credits the channel that started it.",
          "Data-driven / position-based (W-shaped) — accounts for first touch, lead creation, and opportunity creation in a multi-touch B2B journey.",
          "Linear — all touches equal weight regardless of intent.",
        ],
        correct: 2,
        explanation:
          "B2B journeys are long and multi-stakeholder. A position-based / W-shaped model weights the demand-creating first touch, the lead-conversion touch, and the opportunity touch — far more realistic than last/first/linear for B2B.",
        skill: "attribution",
        difficulty: 3,
      },
      {
        id: "a10",
        type: "mcq",
        prompt:
          "Scenario: A landing page loads in 4.8s (LCP), has a CLS of 0.25, and a mobile bounce rate of 78%. Which fix is likely to yield the largest conversion lift?",
        options: [
          "Rewrite the headline to be more clever.",
          "Fix CLS and reduce LCP below 2.5s — performance is core to mobile conversion; 78% bounce on a 4.8s page is a performance problem before it's a copy problem.",
          "Add more testimonials.",
          "Change the button color to red.",
        ],
        correct: 1,
        explanation:
          "78% mobile bounce with 4.8s LCP and 0.25 CLS points to performance as the dominant leak. Fix Core Web Vitals first — conversion-rate copy tweaks are wasted on a page users abandon before reading.",
        skill: "cro",
        difficulty: 2,
      },
    ],
  },
];

export function getExam(id: string): Exam | undefined {
  return EXAMS.find((e) => e.id === id);
}

One concern: in the timer's auto-submit path, `finishExam(true)` is called. But `finishExam` reads from state (`answers`, `shuffled`, `activeExam`, `startTime`) via closure. Since the effect runs on each tick and the closure captures the latest state... actually no, the interval callback captures the closure from when the effect ran (when phase became "running"). So `answers` and `shuffled` would be stale at the time of auto-submit.

Wait, let me think about this more carefully. The effect runs once when `phase` becomes "running" and `activeExam` is set. The `setInterval` callback closes over the `finishExam` function and state from that moment. So if the timer fires 15 minutes later, `answers` would be stale (the initial empty `{}`).

There's a stale closure bug: the timer effect closes over `finishExam`, which closes over `computeResult`, which reads `answers`, `activeExam`, `shuffled`, `candidate`, `startTime`, `history`. Since the effect only depends on `[phase, activeExam]`, the `finishExam(true)` call inside the interval will use the stale `answers` (empty `{}`) captured when the effect ran. This means auto-submit on timeout would score 0.

I need to use refs to always read the latest state. Let me add refs for `answers`, `shuffled`, `candidate`, `startTime`, `history`, and `activeExam`. Actually `activeExam` is in the deps so it's fine. The key stale ones are `answers` and `history`.

data.text:
Expanded the Marketing Tests feature with four upgrades. All typecheck cleanly against the project (the lone remaining project error is pre-existing in `src/lib/intent.ts`, untouched by me).

## What changed

**`src/lib/marketing-tests/exams.ts`** — added a 7th, expert-level exam:
- **Marketing Analytics & Attribution (Scenario Exam)** — 10 situational questions on attribution models, funnel math, CAC/LTV unit economics, A/B test interpretation, conversion diagnostics, and CRO. 80% pass mark, 18 min. Tests applied judgement, not recall.
- Total bank is now **7 exams · 53 questions · 7 domains · 3 levels** (associate / professional / expert).

**`src/components/marketing/MarketingTests.tsx`** — three new features:
1. **Candidate identity** — name (required) + optional email captured before an exam starts, stored in `localStorage` and shown on every report. The Start button is disabled until a name is entered.
2. **Exam timer** — live countdown from `durationMinutes`, dual progress bars (answered % + time left), turns red under 60s, and **auto-submits on expiry** (locks inputs, flags the report as "Timed out — auto-submitted"). Fixed the stale-closure trap: the interval reads latest answers/history via refs, so timeout submissions record the correct partial state instead of empty state.
3. **Results history** — every attempt persists to `localStorage` (last 100). The picker shows a recent-attempts strip; a full "History" tabbed view lists all attempts in a sortable table (candidate, exam, date, duration, score, band, pass/fail) with click-to-reopen any past report.

## Verification
- `tsc --noEmit` (full project): **0 errors** in `marketing-tests/exams.ts`, `MarketingTests.tsx`, or `marketing/page.tsx`.
- The only project-wide TS error (`src/lib/intent.ts:249`) is a pre-existing working-tree modification I did not touch.
- `next build` is still blocked by the pre-existing `smarketing.ts` `avgDealSize` redeclaration, also unrelated to this work.
- No new dependencies; uses existing `@/components/marketing/ui` primitives plus the newly-imported `Input`.

Let me define the type contract precisely:

```ts
export type QuestionType = "mcq" | "truefalse" | "multiselect" | "numeric";

export interface BaseQuestion {
  id: string;
  skill: string;
  difficulty: 1 | 2 | 3;
  explanation: string;
}

export interface MCQQuestion extends BaseQuestion {
  type: "mcq" | "truefalse";
  prompt: string;
  options: string[];
  correct: number; // index
}

export interface MultiSelectQuestion extends BaseQuestion {
  type: "multiselect";
  prompt: string;
  options: string[];
  correct: number[]; // indices, must select ALL and ONLY these
  partialCredit?: boolean; // default true: half credit for partial
}

export interface NumericQuestion extends BaseQuestion {
  type: "numeric";
  prompt: string;
  correct: number;
  tolerance?: number; // absolute tolerance, default 0
  unit?: string;
}

export type Question = MCQQuestion | MultiSelectQuestion | NumericQuestion;
```

For scoring with adaptive weighting:
- Each question scored 0-1 (multiselect partial, numeric within tolerance).
- Weighted by difficulty: difficulty 1 = weight 1, 2 = weight 2, 3 = weight 3.
- Score = sum(earned_weight) / sum(total_weight) * 100.

  difficulty: number;
  earnedWeight: number;
  totalWeight: number;
}
```

I verified the file by careful manual inspection instead.

## Report

### 1. Compilation
I could not run `tsc` directly (the background_process/bash execution path is blocked by permission rules in this environment — `npx tsc *` and `tsc *` are allow-listed for a bash tool that isn't exposed to me). I verified type-correctness by manual inspection against the `tsc --strict` + `noUnusedLocals`/`noUnusedParameters` requirements:

- All interfaces match the required discriminated-union contract exactly.
- Every `numeric` question has `options: []`, a numeric `correct`, and a `tolerance` (20 instances, all confirmed).
- Every `multiselect` question has `correct: number[]` (24 instances, all confirmed).
- Every `truefalse` question has `options: ["True","False"]` and `correct: 0|1`.
- All `difficulty` values are literals `1|2|3` with contextual typing from `Exam[]` (same pattern as the original compiling file).
- No unused locals/parameters: `csvField` and `fnv1aHex` are both used; all function params (`q`, `exam`, `r`, `id`, `score`, `input`, `value`) are referenced.
- No React/next/DOM imports or `window`/`localStorage` usage — pure TS.
- `DIFFICULTY_WEIGHT[q.difficulty]` indexes `Record<Difficulty, number>` with `Difficulty` — valid.
- CSV header column order exactly matches `attemptToCsvRow` field order (verified line-by-line).

**You should run the verification command yourself** to confirm: `cd /home/uzer/marketing-hub && npx tsc --noEmit --strict --jsx preserve --target ES2022 --module esnext --moduleResolution bundler --skipLibCheck --esModuleInterop --lib dom,dom.iterable,esnext src/lib/marketing-tests/exams.ts`

### 2. Counts
- **Exams: 12** — `fundamentals`, `seo`, `email`, `social`, `ppc`, `content`, `analytics`, `brand-strategy`, `growth-retention`, `marketing-ops`, `cro-landing`, `compliance-privacy`
- **Total questions: 148**
- **Breakdown by type:** mcq/truefalse = 104, **multiselect = 24**, **numeric = 20** (verified via grep: 25 `type: "multiselect"` matches − 1 interface line = 24; 21 `type: "numeric"` − 1 interface = 20)
- Each of the 12 exams has ≥1 multiselect and ≥1 numeric, and each has 10–14 questions.

### 3. Exported symbols (all confirmed present)
`QuestionType`, `Difficulty`, `MCQQuestion`, `TrueFalseQuestion`, `MultiSelectQuestion`, `NumericQuestion`, `Question`, `Exam`, `SkillBand`, `SKILL_BANDS`, `bandFor`, `EXAMS`, `getExam`, `DIFFICULTY_WEIGHT`, `examMaxWeight`, `isMultiSelect`, `isNumeric`, `AttemptSummary`, `ATTEMPT_CSV_HEADER`, `attemptToCsvRow`, `certificateId` — plus the required backward-compatible names are all preserved.

### 4. Helper functions section (lines 91–165)

```ts
export const DIFFICULTY_WEIGHT: Record<Difficulty, number> = { 1: 1, 2: 1.5, 3: 2 };

export function examMaxWeight(exam: Exam): number {
  return exam.questions.reduce((sum, q) => sum + DIFFICULTY_WEIGHT[q.difficulty], 0);
}

export function isMultiSelect(q: Question): q is MultiSelectQuestion {
  return q.type === "multiselect";
}

export function isNumeric(q: Question): q is NumericQuestion {
  return q.type === "numeric";
}

export interface AttemptSummary {
  attemptId: string;
  examId: string;
  examTitle: string;
  domain: string;
  candidate: string;
  email: string;
  percentage: number;
  weightedPercentage: number;
  band: string;
  passed: boolean;
  elapsedSeconds: number;
  timedOut: boolean;
  tabSwitches: number;
  takenAt: number;
}

export const ATTEMPT_CSV_HEADER: string =
  "attemptId,examId,examTitle,domain,candidate,email,percentage,weightedPercentage,band,passed,elapsedSeconds,timedOut,tabSwitches,takenAt";

function csvField(value: string): string {
  const needsQuoting = /[",\n\r]/.test(value);
  if (!needsQuoting) return value;
  return '"' + value.replace(/"/g, '""') + '"';
}

export function attemptToCsvRow(r: AttemptSummary): string {
  return [
    csvField(r.attemptId),
    csvField(r.examId),
    csvField(r.examTitle),
    csvField(r.domain),
    csvField(r.candidate),
    csvField(r.email),
    String(r.percentage),
    String(r.weightedPercentage),
    csvField(r.band),
    String(r.passed),
    String(r.elapsedSeconds),
    String(r.timedOut),
    String(r.tabSwitches),
    String(r.takenAt),
  ].join(",");
}

// FNV-1a 32-bit → 8 hex chars. Deterministic for identical inputs.
function fnv1aHex(input: string): string {
  let hash = 0x811c9dc5;
  for (let i = 0; i < input.length; i++) {
    hash ^= input.charCodeAt(i);
    // FNV prime multiplication, keep within 32-bit unsigned.
    hash = Math.imul(hash, 0x01000193) >>> 0;
  }
  const hex = (hash >>> 0).toString(16);
  return hex.padStart(8, "0").slice(0, 8);
}

export function certificateId(examId: string, candidate: string, takenAt: number): string {
  const seed = `${examId}|${candidate}|${takenAt}`;
  return `MKT-${fnv1aHex(seed)}`;
}
```

`certificateId("seo", "Jane Doe", 1720000000)` → deterministic `MKT-XXXXXXXX` (8 hex chars from FNV-1a 32-bit over the seed string). Same inputs always produce the same id.
</task_result>
</task>

input.prompt:
You are UPGRADING an existing React client component in a Next.js marketing-hub project. This is the central integration piece of a "push to the limit" expansion of a recruiting-grade marketing-exams feature.

## File to OVERWRITE (existing file — read it first, then rewrite)
`/home/uzer/marketing-hub/src/components/marketing/MarketingTests.tsx`

READ the current file first so you understand the existing structure (phases, timer, history, refs for stale-closure fix). You must PRESERVE the working behaviors: candidate identity capture, countdown timer with auto-submit, localStorage history persistence, the stale-closure ref pattern (answersRef/shuffledRef/candidateRef/startTimeRef/historyRef/activeExamRef), and the print button.

## The new data contract (from `@/lib/marketing-tests/exams` — already written by another agent)
```ts
export type QuestionType = "mcq" | "truefalse" | "multiselect" | "numeric";
export type Difficulty = 1 | 2 | 3;
export interface MCQQuestion { id; type:"mcq"; prompt; options:string[]; correct:number; explanation; skill; difficulty }
export interface TrueFalseQuestion { id; type:"truefalse"; prompt; options:string[]; correct:number; explanation; skill; difficulty }
export interface MultiSelectQuestion { id; type:"multiselect"; prompt; options:string[]; correct:number[]; explanation; skill; difficulty }
export interface NumericQuestion { id; type:"numeric"; prompt; options:[]; correct:number; tolerance:number; unit?:string; explanation; skill; difficulty }
export type Question = MCQQuestion | TrueFalseQuestion | MultiSelectQuestion | NumericQuestion;
export interface Exam { id; title; domain; description; durationMinutes; passingScore; level:"associate"|"professional"|"expert"; questions: Question[] }
export interface SkillBand { min; max; band; label }
export const SKILL_BANDS; export function bandFor(score:number):SkillBand;
export const EXAMS: Exam[]; export function getExam(id:string): Exam|undefined;
export const DIFFICULTY_WEIGHT: Record<Difficulty, number>;  // {1:1, 2:1.5, 3:2}
export function examMaxWeight(exam: Exam): number;
export function isMultiSelect(q: Question): q is MultiSelectQuestion;
export function isNumeric(q: Question): q is NumericQuestion;
export interface AttemptSummary { ... }  // for export
export const ATTEMPT_CSV_HEADER: string;
export function attemptToCsvRow(r: AttemptSummary): string;
export function certificateId(examId: string, candidate: string, takenAt: number): string;
```

## Sibling components being built in parallel (import them)
```ts
import { ExamCertificate } from "@/components/marketing/ExamCertificate";
import type { ExamResult } from "@/components/marketing/ExamCertificate";   // CANONICAL ExamResult type — USE THIS, do not redefine
import { RecruiterCompare } from "@/components/marketing/RecruiterCompare";
```
`ExamResult` (canonical, imported from ExamCertificate) has shape:
```ts
interface ExamResult {
  attemptId: string; examId: string; examTitle: string; domain: string;
  score: number; total: number; correctCount: number; percentage: number;
  passed: boolean; band: SkillBand; skillBreakdown: Record<string,{correct,total,pct}>;
  answers: AnswerRecord[]; candidate: string; email: string; elapsedSeconds: number;
  timedOut: boolean; tabSwitches: number; takenAt: number;
}
```
Note the NEW field `tabSwitches: number` vs the old version. Your `AnswerRecord` interface you define locally; `answers: AnswerRecord[]`.

## UI primitives (same as current file)
`@/components/marketing/ui`: `Card`, `Button`, `Badge`, `Input`.

## What to upgrade (the "limit" features)

### 1. New question types in the runner
The runner must handle all 4 question types via the discriminated union:
- **mcq / truefalse**: render option buttons as today (single select).
- **multiselect**: render checkboxes (toggle each option). Store answer as `Record<questionId, number[]>` — BUT the existing `answers` state is `Record<string, number>`. CHANGE the answers state to a union store: `Record<string, number | number[]>` where mcq/truefalse/numeric store a `number` and multiselect stores `number[]`. Update `selectAnswer` accordingly and add `toggleMultiAnswer(questionId, optionIdx)`.
- **numeric**: render an `Input type="number"`. Store the numeric value (number). Show the `unit` suffix if present.

### 2. Adaptive weighted scoring in computeResult
Replace simple correct-count scoring with difficulty-weighted scoring:
- For each question, compute `earnedWeight`: if correct → `DIFFICULTY_WEIGHT[q.difficulty]`, else 0. For multiselect, award PARTIAL credit: `partialFraction = (correctlySelected ∩ correct.length) ... ` — specifically: count true positives (selected ∩ correct) minus false positives (selected ∉ correct), clamped to [0,1], times the weight. If the clamp gives <0, award 0. If all correct and no extras, full weight.
- `weightedPercentage = round(earnedWeightSum / examMaxWeight(exam) * 100)`.
- Keep `percentage` (raw correct/total) too, but use `weightedPercentage` for pass/fail against `exam.passingScore` AND for `bandFor(...)`.
- Record per-question `earnedWeight` in AnswerRecord.

### 3. Anti-cheat: tab-switch detection
- Add state `tabSwitches` (number) and a `tabSwitchesRef` (mirror via useEffect like the other refs).
- In a `useEffect` active only during `phase === "running"`, listen to `document` `visibilitychange` (and window `blur` as backup). When the document becomes hidden (`document.hidden` true) or window blurs, increment `tabSwitches` and `tabSwitchesRef.current`.
- Show a live counter in the running header: "Tab switches: {n}" with a warning style (amber/red) when >0. Show a warning banner above the question when tabSwitches >= 3: "Repeated tab switching detected — this is recorded on your report."
- Include `tabSwitches` in the computed ExamResult (read from `tabSwitchesRef.current` so the timer's stale-closure auto-submit gets the latest count).
- Disable inputs on timeout as before.

### 4. Printable certificate on results
- On the results view, if `result.passed`, render `<ExamCertificate result={result} />` ABOVE the existing recruiting report card.
- If failed, ExamCertificate handles its own "not earned" notice (still render it — it self-gates).

### 5. Recruiter compare view
- Add a new phase `"compare"`. Add a "Recruiter Compare" button on the picker (next to/replacing the existing "View all" history button). When clicked, set phase to "compare" and render `<RecruiterCompare history={history} onBack={() => setPhase("picker")} onViewAttempt={(a) => { setViewingHistory(a); setPhase("results"); }} />`.
- Keep the existing history view too (phase "history"). The picker can have two buttons: "History" (→ phase history) and "Recruiter Compare" (→ phase compare).

### 6. Keep everything that works
- Candidate identity (name required, email optional), localStorage candidate persistence.
- Countdown timer with auto-submit on expiry, dual progress bars, red under 60s, timeout flag on report.
- localStorage history persistence (last 100), recent-attempts strip on picker, full history table view.
- The stale-closure ref pattern (answersRef, shuffledRef, candidateRef, startTimeRef, historyRef, activeExamRef) — ADD tabSwitchesRef to it.
- Print button.
- Answer review with explanations on results.

## AnswerRecord (define locally, used in ExamResult.answers)
```ts
interface AnswerRecord {
  questionId: string;
  type: QuestionType;
  selected: number | number[];   // number for mcq/truefalse/numeric, number[] for multiselect
  correct: number | number[];
  skill: string;
  difficulty: Difficulty;
  isCorrect: boolean;            // for partial credit, isCorrect = earnedFraction >= 1
  earnedWeight: number;          // weighted points earned
  maxWeight: number;             // DIFFICULTY_WEIGHT[difficulty]
}
```
For the answer review rendering, handle multiselect (show checkboxes state, mark correct set) and numeric (show entered value vs correct±tolerance).

## Hard constraints
- `"use client";` at top.
- TypeScript strict-compatible. No `any`. No unused imports/vars/params.
- Only `react` + the three import paths (`@/lib/marketing-tests/exams`, `@/components/marketing/ui`, `@/components/marketing/ExamCertificate`, `@/components/marketing/RecruiterCompare`). No new deps.
- Must compile under `tsc --strict` with noUnusedLocals/noUnusedParameters.
- `window`/`document` usage allowed (client component — needed for visibilitychange, blur, localStorage, print).
- Preserve the stale-closure ref pattern so timer auto-submit reads latest state.
- For the discriminated union, narrow with `q.type === "multiselect"` / `q.type === "numeric"` / `isMultiSelect(q)` / `isNumeric(q)` so TS knows the field shapes. CRITICAL: when iterating `q.options` for a numeric question, `options` is `[]` so the options.map renders nothing — that's why you branch on type BEFORE mapping options.

You cannot run tsc directly (bash blocked for subagents). Self-review EXTREMELY carefully for:
- Discriminated-union narrowing (the #1 source of errors). Use `if (q.type === "numeric") {...} else if (q.type === "multiselect") {...} else {...}` branches in both the runner and the answer review.
- The answers state type `Record<string, number | number[]>` and that computeResult reads `ans[q.id]` then narrows by `q.type`.
- Partial-credit multiselect math.
- All refs mirrored.
- `tabSwitches` included in the returned ExamResult.
- ExamResult matches the canonical shape from ExamCertificate (do NOT redefine ExamResult — import the type).
- No unused imports (e.g. if you import `isMultiSelect`/`isNumeric`/`examMaxWeight`/`DIFFICULTY_WEIGHT`/`certificateId` — make sure each is used; `certificateId` is used inside ExamCertificate, NOT here, so do NOT import it here unless you use it).

## Report back
1. Confirm file overwritten + all 6 upgrade features present.
2. Confirm the answers state type and how multiselect/numeric are stored.
3. Confirm weighted scoring math (paste the partial-credit multiselect block).
4. Confirm anti-cheat effect (visibilitychange + blur) and that tabSwitchesRef feeds the result.
5. Confirm ExamCertificate renders on passed results and RecruiterCompare is wired.
6. Paste the computeResult function body so I can verify the scoring.
